CRT exponent targets with a prime square and an auxiliary prime

Let $q=109$ and $\ell=101$. Assume that $6$ is a primitive root modulo $q^2$ and that $2$ is a primitive root modulo $\ell$.

Let $B$ be the least nonnegative integer such that $B \equiv 6^{135} \pmod{q^2}$, and let $C$ be the least nonnegative integer such that $C \equiv 2^{39} \pmod{\ell}$.

Let $A$ be the least nonnegative integer satisfying the simultaneous congruences
$A \equiv 4 \pmod{3^6}$, $A \equiv 6 \pmod{q^2}$, and $A \equiv 2 \pmod{\ell}$.

Let $n_0$ be the least positive integer $n$ such that
$A^n \equiv 1 \pmod{3^6}$, $A^n \equiv B \pmod{q^2}$, and $A^n \equiv C \pmod{\ell}$.

Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0 \le N < 1000$ and $N \equiv n_0 \pmod{1000}$.

Solution.
Step 1 (Chinese_remainder).
The moduli $3^6$, $q^2$, and $\ell$ are pairwise coprime, so the Chinese remainder theorem gives a unique residue class modulo $3^6 q^2 \ell$ satisfying
$A \equiv 4 \pmod{3^6}$, $A \equiv 6 \pmod{q^2}$, $A \equiv 2 \pmod{\ell}$.
Taking the least nonnegative representative defines $A$ uniquely.

Step 2 (modular_reduction).
From the defining congruences, $A \equiv 4 \not\equiv 0 \pmod{3}$, $A \equiv 6 \not\equiv 0 \pmod{q}$, and $A \equiv 2 \not\equiv 0 \pmod{\ell}$, hence $\gcd(A,3^6)=\gcd(A,q^2)=\gcd(A,\ell)=1$.
Therefore $A$ is a unit modulo each modulus, so we can use multiplicative orders and reduce the three conditions independently modulo $3^6$, $q^2$, and $\ell$.

Lemma 1.
For every integer $n \ge 1$, $v_3(4^n-1)=1+v_3(n)$.
Proof.
Let $p=3$, $a=4$, $b=1$. Then $p$ is odd, $p \mid a-b$ since $4-1=3$, and $p \nmid ab$ since $3 \nmid 4$.
By LTE, $v_3(4^n-1^n)=v_3(4-1)+v_3(n)=1+v_3(n)$.

Step 3 (LTE_valuation and multiplicative_order).
We determine the exact condition for $4^n \equiv 1 \pmod{3^6}$.
This congruence is equivalent to $3^6 \mid 4^n-1$, which is equivalent to $v_3(4^n-1) \ge 6$.
By Lemma 1, this is equivalent to $1+v_3(n) \ge 6$, i.e. $v_3(n) \ge 5$, i.e. $3^5 \mid n$.
In particular, $4^{3^5} \equiv 1 \pmod{3^6}$, and $4^{3^4} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{3^6}$, so $\operatorname{ord}_{3^6}(4)=3^5=243$.

Step 4 (order_translation).
Since $A \equiv 4 \pmod{3^6}$, $A$ and $4$ represent the same element of $(\mathbb Z/3^6\mathbb Z)^\times$, hence $\operatorname{ord}_{3^6}(A)=\operatorname{ord}_{3^6}(4)=243$.
Therefore
$A^n \equiv 1 \pmod{3^6}$ if and only if $243 \mid n$.                                     (1)

Lemma 2.
Let $q$ be an odd prime and let $g$ be a primitive root modulo $q^2$.
Then $\varphi(q^2)=q(q-1)$, $\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(g)=q(q-1)$, and for all integers $n,r$,
$g^n \equiv g^r \pmod{q^2}$ if and only if $n \equiv r \pmod{q(q-1)}$.
Proof.
We have $\varphi(q^2)=q^2-q=q(q-1)$.
Since $g$ is a primitive root modulo $q^2$, it generates $(\mathbb Z/q^2\mathbb Z)^\times$, so $\operatorname{ord}_{q^2}(g)=\varphi(q^2)=q(q-1)$.
In a cyclic group generated by $g$ of order $m$, $g^n=g^r$ holds if and only if $m \mid (n-r)$, i.e. $n \equiv r \pmod m$.
Taking $m=q(q-1)$ gives the claim.

Step 5 (primitive_root_mod_prime_power and cyclic_exponent_identification).
Here $q=109$ and $g=6$ is a primitive root modulo $q^2$, so by Lemma 2 the order of $6$ modulo $q^2$ is $q(q-1)=109 \cdot 108=11772$.
Also $B \equiv 6^{135} \pmod{q^2}$ by definition and $A \equiv 6 \pmod{q^2}$ by construction, so $A^n \equiv B \pmod{q^2}$ is equivalent to $6^n \equiv 6^{135} \pmod{q^2}$.
By Lemma 2 this is equivalent to
$n \equiv 135 \pmod{11772}$.                                               (2)

Step 6 (primitive_root_usage).
Since $2$ is a primitive root modulo the prime $\ell=101$, we have $\operatorname{ord}_{\ell}(2)=\ell-1=100$.
Because $C \equiv 2^{39} \pmod{\ell}$ and $A \equiv 2 \pmod{\ell}$, the condition $A^n \equiv C \pmod{\ell}$ is equivalent to $2^n \equiv 2^{39} \pmod{\ell}$, hence to
$n \equiv 39 \pmod{100}$.                                                  (3)

Step 7 (gcd_based_reduction and linear_congruence_solvability).
From (1) write $n=243t$ with $t \in \mathbb Z_{>0}$.
Substituting into (2) gives
$243t \equiv 135 \pmod{11772}$.
Let $d=\gcd(243,11772)$. Since $243=3^5$ and $11772=109 \cdot 108=109 \cdot 2^2 \cdot 3^3$, we have $d=3^3=27$.
Because $27 \mid 135$, the congruence is solvable, and dividing by $27$ yields the equivalent congruence
$9t \equiv 5 \pmod{436}$.
Since $\gcd(9,436)=1$, $9$ has a multiplicative inverse modulo $436$.
Indeed $9 \cdot 97=873 \equiv 1 \pmod{436}$, so $9^{-1} \equiv 97 \pmod{436}$, and multiplying by $97$ gives
$t \equiv 5 \cdot 97 \equiv 485 \equiv 49 \pmod{436}$.
Thus all solutions to (1) and (2) are
$t=49+436u$ with $u \in \mathbb Z_{\ge 0}$, hence
$n=243t=243(49+436u)=11907+105948u$.                                       (4)

Step 8 (generalized_CRT_sequential and linear_congruence_solvability).
Impose (3) on the parametrization (4):
$11907+105948u \equiv 39 \pmod{100}$.
Reducing modulo $100$ gives $7+48u \equiv 39 \pmod{100}$, so
$48u \equiv 32 \pmod{100}$.
Let $d_2=\gcd(48,100)=4$. Since $4 \mid 32$, this is solvable, and dividing by $4$ gives
$12u \equiv 8 \pmod{25}$.
Now $\gcd(12,25)=1$, and $12 \cdot 23=276 \equiv 1 \pmod{25}$, so $12^{-1} \equiv 23 \pmod{25}$.
Multiplying by $23$ gives
$u \equiv 8 \cdot 23 \equiv 184 \equiv 9 \pmod{25}$.                       (5)

Step 9 (extremal_argument).
The smallest nonnegative $u$ satisfying (5) is $u_0=9$.
Substituting $u_0$ into (4) gives the least positive solution
$n_0=11907+105948 \cdot 9=965439$.

Step 10 (modular_exponentiation).
Compute $N \equiv n_0 \pmod{1000}$.
We have $n_0 \equiv 11907+105948 \cdot 9 \pmod{1000}$, so
$n_0 \equiv 907+948 \cdot 9 \equiv 907+8532 \equiv 907+532 \equiv 1439 \equiv 439 \pmod{1000}$.
Thus the required integer $N$ with $0 \le N < 1000$ is $N=439$.

3. Uniqueness Test
Any $n$ satisfying $A^n \equiv 1 \pmod{3^6}$ must satisfy $243 \mid n$ because $\operatorname{ord}_{3^6}(A)=243$ from Step 4, so write $n=243t$.
Any $n$ satisfying $A^n \equiv B \pmod{q^2}$ must satisfy $n \equiv 135 \pmod{11772}$ from Step 5, hence $243t \equiv 135 \pmod{11772}$.
Step 7 shows this has the unique solution class $t \equiv 49 \pmod{436}$, so the first two constraints force exactly the arithmetic progression $n=11907+105948u$.
Imposing the third constraint $A^n \equiv C \pmod{\ell}$ is equivalent to $n \equiv 39 \pmod{100}$ from Step 6, which by Step 8 forces the unique solution class $u \equiv 9 \pmod{25}$.
Therefore all solutions to the full system are
$n=11907+105948(9+25v)=965439+2648700v$ with $v \in \mathbb Z_{\ge 0}$.
This set has a unique smallest element $n_0=965439$, so $N \equiv n_0 \pmod{1000}$ with $0 \le N < 1000$ is uniquely determined, and equals $439$.

439
